conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Hello! I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. Let me start by gathering some information from you.

First, could you please tell me: Do you have any children, and if so, how many children are you inquiring about?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hello! Yes, I'd like to find out if I'm eligible for Child Benefit. I have one child - my daughter Alex, she's 8 years old.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information! To help determine your eligibility for Child Benefit for Alex, I need to ask you a few questions.

First, could you tell me: Are you responsible for Alex? That is, do you live with her and are you responsible for paying for things like her food, clothes, and other needs?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Well, Alex doesn't actually live with me at the moment. She lives with her dad. I do help out a bit financially when I can - I send money for things like clothes and bits and pieces for her.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. Since Alex lives with her dad, I need to understand the situation a bit better.

Could you tell me: Does Alex's dad currently claim Child Benefit for her?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, her dad doesn't claim Child Benefit for Alex.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for clarifying that. This is helpful information.

Let me ask: How often do you see Alex or have contact with her? And approximately how much financial support do you provide for her - is it regular support, or occasional contributions?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: I see her regularly - we keep in touch. As for the financial support, I send her dad about £10 a week to help with things she needs.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for providing those details. Let me gather just a bit more information to make sure I have a complete picture.

You mentioned Alex lives with her dad. Could you tell me: Is this a permanent arrangement, or is it temporary? And are you and her dad sharing the responsibility for raising Alex, or is her dad the main person responsible for her day-to-day care?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: It's a permanent arrangement - her dad is the main person responsible for her day-to-day care. He's the one looking after her full-time and handling all her daily needs.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for explaining the situation. Let me just ask one final question to be thorough:

Are you currently living in the UK, and is Alex also living in the UK?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I live in the UK - in Birmingham. And yes, Alex is also living in the UK with her dad.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for providing all that information. Let me now assess your eligibility for Child Benefit for Alex based on the rules.

Based on what you've told me:
- Alex is 8 years old and lives permanently with her dad
- Her dad is responsible for her day-to-day care
- You contribute approximately £10 per week (about £40 per month)
- Her dad does not currently claim Child Benefit for Alex
- Both you and Alex live in the UK

Let me calculate the current Child Benefit rate to compare with your contributions. The current Child Benefit rate for the eldest or only child is £25.60 per week (or about £102.40 per month).

Your weekly contribution of £10 is unfortunately less than the Child Benefit payment amount of £25.60 per week.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Based on my assessment, **you are not eligible for Child Benefit for Alex**.

Here's why: To qualify for Child Benefit when your child doesn't live with you, you need to be paying at least the same amount as the Child Benefit payment towards looking after them. The current Child Benefit rate is £25.60 per week for an only or eldest child. While you do contribute £10 per week towards Alex's upkeep, this is less than the Child Benefit amount.

Since Alex lives permanently with her dad and he's responsible for her day-to-day care, **her dad would be the eligible person to claim Child Benefit** for Alex. You may want to let him know that he can make a claim, as he mentioned he's not currently claiming it.

Is there anything else you'd like to know about Child Benefit?

claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 1 child:
  - Alex is 8 years old and does not live with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 8 (under 16)
  - Child does not live with claimant and weekly upkeep (£10.00) is below the Child Benefit rate (£26.05)
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Child does not live with claimant and weekly upkeep (£10.00) is below the Child Benefit rate (£26.05)
circumstances: Child does not live with claimant and weekly upkeep (£10.00) is below the Child Benefit rate (£26.05)
Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

reasonings: According to UK Child Benefit rules, you normally qualify for Child Benefit if you're responsible for a child under 16 (or under 20 if in approved education). You're usually considered responsible if either: (1) you live with the child, or (2) you're paying at least the same amount as Child Benefit towards looking after them. Alex lives permanently with her dad, who is responsible for her day-to-day care, so condition (1) is not met. You contribute £10 per week towards Alex's upkeep, but the current Child Benefit rate for an only/eldest child is £25.60 per week. Since your contribution (£10/week) is less than the Child Benefit amount (£25.60/week), condition (2) is not met either. Therefore, you do not meet the eligibility criteria for Child Benefit for Alex. Her dad would be the eligible person to claim as he lives with her and is responsible for her care.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant has one daughter (Alex, age 8) who lives permanently with her father. The claimant contributes £10 per week but does not live with the child. Since the contribution (£10/week) is less than the Child Benefit rate (£25.60/week) and the child doesn't live with the claimant, the claimant does not meet the eligibility requirements. The child's father would be the eligible person to claim as the main carer.
